topic: Imperfect past of ser and estar

tips.title: Same split, just in the past
tips.body: The ser/estar divide you learned for the present carries straight into the past: era describes what something was (identity, description, origin), while estaba describes where something was or how it felt (location, condition, emotion).

tips.title: Era is irregular, estaba is not
tips.body: Estaba follows the normal imperfect pattern for -ar verbs, so once you learn the pattern it's predictable. Ser, though, is one of only two verbs in all of Spanish (along with ir) that is fully irregular in the imperfect — era, eras, era, éramos, eran just have to be memorized.

tips.title: Imperfect paints the background of a story
tips.body: Unlike a tense that reports a single finished action, the imperfect describes ongoing states or scenery in the past: what things were like, how someone felt, where something was. It answers 'what was going on' rather than 'what happened.'

tips.title: Don't drop the accent on éramos
tips.body: Éramos ('we were') is the only form of era that carries a written accent mark. It's a small detail, but it's an easy one to forget when you're writing quickly.

bonusCards.english: I was tired, and I was in bed all day.
bonusCards.answer: I **estaba** tired, **y** I **estaba** **en** bed all day.

bonusCards.english: The teacher was very kind, and the students were happy.
bonusCards.answer: **El** teacher **era** very kind, **y** the students **estaban** happy.

bonusCards.english: We were friends, and we were always together.
Why were you here? I was looking for the keys.
The house was old, but it was clean.
